summary:
Hadrian's Wall (begun 122 AD) was the most northerly and most impressive of Rome's fixed frontier defenses — an 80-mile (117 km) stone and turf wall across the narrow waist of northern Britain from the Solway Firth to the Tyne estuary, built on the orders of Emperor Hadrian to mark and defend the northern limit of Roman Britain. It was not merely a wall but an integrated frontier system: a continuous barrier with milecastles (small forts at every Roman mile) and signal towers between them, backed by a series of large auxiliary forts.

Hadrian's Wall exemplifies the shift from Trajan's expansionist strategy to Hadrian's consolidationist approach: rather than seeking to conquer the unconquerable Scottish Highlands, Hadrian defined and fortified the empire's limits. The wall required an estimated 3 million cubic yards of stone and earth and was built largely by legionary soldiers (who inscribed their sections with unit identifications still visible today). At its military peak, perhaps 9,000–10,000 troops were stationed along the wall. It remained in use until the Roman withdrawal from Britain in the early 5th century. Today it is a UNESCO World Heritage Site and the best-preserved example of Roman frontier military engineering in the world.
